title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: global-scale species distribution models climate fields, need mesh sphere. tulpa_mesh_sphere() generates one recursive subdivision icosahedron. subdivision level quadruples triangle count: vertices lie exactly sphere surface:
code: globe <- tulpa_mesh_sphere(subdivisions = 3) globe #> tulpa_mesh (sphere, radius = 1 ): #>   Vertices:   642  #>   Triangles:  1280  #>   Edges:      1920 radii <- sqrt(rowSums(globe$vertices^2)) range(radii) #> [1] 1 1

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: 3D FEM assembly uses metric tensor triangle’s tangent plane:
code:
fem <- fem_matrices(globe, lumped = TRUE)  # Total surface area should approximate 4*pi for unit sphere cat("Total area:", sum(fem$va), "
") #> Total area: 12.50649 cat("4*pi:      ", 4 * pi, "
") #> 4*pi:       12.56637 cat("Error:     ", abs(sum(fem$va) - 4 * pi) / (4 * pi) * 100, "%
") #> Error:      0.476493 %

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: Pass observation coordinates (longitude, latitude) degrees:
code: obs <- cbind(lon = c(0, 90, -45, 170), lat = c(0, 45, -30, 60)) fem <- fem_matrices(globe, obs_coords = obs) dim(fem$A) #> [1]   4 642 range(Matrix::rowSums(fem$A))  # row sums = 1 #> [1] 1 1

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
code: earth <- tulpa_mesh_sphere(subdivisions = 3, radius = 6371) sqrt(sum(earth$vertices[1, ]^2))  # 6371 km #> [1] 6371

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: closed sphere satisfies V - E + T = 2:
code: globe$n_vertices - globe$n_edges + globe$n_triangles #> [1] 2

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: spatio-temporal SPDE models, temporal dimension needs mesh. tulpa_mesh_1d() produces knots tridiagonal FEM matrices combine spatial mesh via Kronecker products. extension knots (controlled n_extend) prevent boundary effects:
code: # Monthly observations over 5 years times <- seq(2020, 2025, by = 1/12) m1d <- tulpa_mesh_1d(times) m1d #> tulpa_mesh_1d: #>   Knots:     67  #>   Range:    [2019.75, 2025.25] #>   Intervals: 66 range(times)       # data range #> [1] 2020 2025 range(m1d$knots)   # mesh range (extended) #> [1] 2019.75 2025.25

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: Mass stiffness matrices tridiagonal sparse:
code: # No extension for cleaner demonstration m <- tulpa_mesh_1d(seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), n_extend = 0)  # Symmetric, positive definite mass Matrix::isSymmetric(m$C) #> [1] TRUE all(Matrix::diag(m$C) > 0) #> [1] TRUE  # Stiffness row sums = 0 max(abs(Matrix::rowSums(m$G))) #> [1] 1.776357e-15  # Total mass = domain length sum(m$C) #> [1] 1

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: 1D mesh handles irregular intervals naturally:
code: # Denser sampling in summer, sparser in winter times_irr <- c(   seq(2020, 2020.25, by = 1/52),      # weekly Jan-Mar   seq(2020.25, 2020.75, by = 1/365),   # daily Apr-Sep   seq(2020.75, 2021, by = 1/52)        # weekly Oct-Dec ) m_irr <- tulpa_mesh_1d(times_irr, n_extend = 0) cat(m_irr$n, "knots from", length(times_irr), "unique time points\n") #> 210 knots from 211 unique time points

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: SPDE models along roads, rivers, coastlines, tulpa_mesh_graph() builds 1D FEM mesh along network edges.
code: # Simple river network: main channel + tributary edges <- list(   cbind(x = seq(0, 10, by = 0.5), y = rep(0, 21)),     # main channel   cbind(x = c(5, 5, 5, 5), y = c(0, 2, 4, 6))          # tributary )  g <- tulpa_mesh_graph(edges) g #> tulpa_mesh_graph: #>   Vertices:  24  #>   Segments:  23  #>   Junctions: 1   Endpoints: 3

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: Nodes edges meet automatically detected:
code: cat("Junctions (degree > 2):", sum(g$degree > 2), "\n") #> Junctions (degree > 2): 1 cat("Endpoints (degree = 1):", sum(g$degree == 1), "\n") #> Endpoints (degree = 1): 3

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: graph FEM matrices structurally identical 1D case assembled across full network:
code: Matrix::isSymmetric(g$C) #> [1] TRUE max(abs(Matrix::rowSums(g$G)))  # row sums ~ 0 #> [1] 0

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
code: g_fine <- tulpa_mesh_graph(edges, max_edge = 0.3) cat("Coarse:", g$n_vertices, "vertices\n") #> Coarse: 24 vertices cat("Fine:  ", g_fine$n_vertices, "vertices\n") #> Fine:   62 vertices

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
code: library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.13.1, GDAL 3.11.4, PROJ 9.7.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE line1 <- st_linestring(cbind(c(0, 5, 10), c(0, 3, 0))) line2 <- st_linestring(cbind(c(5, 5), c(3, 8))) g_sf <- tulpa_mesh_graph(st_sfc(line1, line2), max_edge = 1) g_sf #> tulpa_mesh_graph: #>   Vertices:  18  #>   Segments:  17  #>   Junctions: 1   Endpoints: 3

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: fields range variance changes across space, fem_matrices_nonstationary() computes weighted FEM matrices C++: constant kappa/tau, weighted matrices simply scaled versions standard ones:
code: set.seed(42) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(cbind(runif(50), runif(50)), max_edge = 0.15) n <- mesh$n_vertices  # Range decreases from left to right kappa <- sqrt(8) / (2 - mesh$vertices[, 1])  # shorter range on the right tau <- rep(1, n)  ns <- fem_matrices_nonstationary(mesh, kappa, tau) names(ns) #> [1] "Ck"     "Gk"     "Ct"     "C"      "G"      "C0"     "n_mesh" ns_const <- fem_matrices_nonstationary(mesh, rep(2, n), rep(3, n)) fem <- fem_matrices(mesh)  max(abs(ns_const$Ck - 4 * fem$C))  # kappa^2 = 4 #> [1] 0 max(abs(ns_const$Ct - 9 * fem$C))  # tau^2 = 9 #> [1] 2.775558e-17

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: higher accuracy fewer mesh nodes, use 6-node quadratic elements: total area preserved:
code:
set.seed(42) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(cbind(runif(30), runif(30))) p2 <- fem_matrices_p2(mesh)  cat("P1 nodes:", mesh$n_vertices, "
") #> P1 nodes: 40 cat("P2 nodes:", p2$n_mesh, "(", p2$n_vertices, "vertices +",     p2$n_midpoints, "midpoints)
") #> P2 nodes: 147 ( 40 vertices + 107 midpoints) fem_p1 <- fem_matrices(mesh) cat("P1 total area:", sum(fem_p1$C), "
") #> P1 total area: 0.9660631 cat("P2 total area:", sum(p2$C), "
") #> P2 total area: 0.9660631

title: Spherical and Temporal Meshes
text: large meshes (>50K triangles), enable parallel assembly:
code: set.seed(42) mesh_large <- tulpa_mesh(cbind(runif(500), runif(500)), max_edge = 0.03) cat(mesh_large$n_triangles, "triangles\n") #> 4014 triangles  fem_seq <- fem_matrices(mesh_large, parallel = FALSE) fem_par <- fem_matrices(mesh_large, parallel = TRUE)  # Results are identical max(abs(fem_seq$C - fem_par$C)) #> [1] 2.168404e-19

title: Quick Start
text: tulpaMesh takes point coordinates returns triangulated mesh FEM matrices ready SPDE models. mesh extends slightly beyond convex hull points (controlled extend). Plot :
code: set.seed(42) coords <- cbind(x = runif(100), y = runif(100)) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(coords) mesh #> tulpa_mesh: #>   Vertices:   113  #>   Triangles:  211  #>   Edges:      323 plot(mesh, vertex_col = "steelblue", main = "Basic mesh")

title: Quick Start
text: Use max_edge add refinement points. mesh generator places hexagonal lattice points spacing, producing near-equilateral triangles.
code: mesh_fine <- tulpa_mesh(coords, max_edge = 0.08) mesh_fine #> tulpa_mesh: #>   Vertices:   337  #>   Triangles:  579  #>   Edges:      875 plot(mesh_fine, main = "Refined mesh (max_edge = 0.08)")

title: Quick Start
text: fem_matrices() returns three sparse matrices needed SPDE models: C: mass matrix (consistent, symmetric positive definite) G: stiffness matrix (symmetric, zero row sums) : projection matrix mapping mesh vertices observation locations SPDE Q-builder, typically need lumped (diagonal) mass matrix:
code: fem <- fem_matrices(mesh_fine, obs_coords = coords) dim(fem$C) #> [1] 337 337 dim(fem$A) #> [1] 100 337  # Verify key properties all(Matrix::diag(fem$C) > 0)        # positive diagonal #> [1] FALSE max(abs(Matrix::rowSums(fem$G)))     # row sums ~ 0 #> [1] 2.842171e-14 range(Matrix::rowSums(fem$A))        # row sums = 1 #> [1] 1 1 fem_l <- fem_matrices(mesh_fine, obs_coords = coords, lumped = TRUE) Matrix::isDiagonal(fem_l$C0) #> [1] TRUE

title: Quick Start
text: coordinates live data.frame, use formula:
code: df <- data.frame(lon = runif(50), lat = runif(50), y = rnorm(50)) mesh_f <- tulpa_mesh(~ lon + lat, data = df) mesh_f #> tulpa_mesh: #>   Vertices:   60  #>   Triangles:  108  #>   Edges:      167

title: Quick Start
text: Check triangle quality mesh_quality() mesh_summary(): Color triangles minimum angle:
code: mesh_summary(mesh_fine) #> Mesh quality summary (579 triangles): #>   Min angle:     min=0.4  median=36.9  max=60.0 deg  #>   Max angle:     min=60.0  median=81.7  max=179.2 deg  #>   Aspect ratio:  min=1.00  median=1.22  max=9736.11   (1 = equilateral) #>   Area:          min=2.36e-05  median=1.69e-03  max=1.51e-02  #>   Warning: 118 triangles with min angle < 20 deg (20.4%) plot(mesh_fine, color = "quality", main = "Colored by minimum angle")

title: Quick Start
text: guaranteed minimum angles, use min_angle:
code: mesh_r <- tulpa_mesh(coords, min_angle = 25, max_edge = 0.15) mesh_summary(mesh_r) #> Mesh quality summary (331 triangles): #>   Min angle:     min=3.2  median=28.4  max=60.0 deg  #>   Max angle:     min=60.0  median=90.8  max=168.5 deg  #>   Aspect ratio:  min=1.00  median=1.48  max=51.82   (1 = equilateral) #>   Area:          min=2.35e-05  median=2.60e-03  max=3.42e-02  #>   Warning: 93 triangles with min angle < 20 deg (28.1%)

title: Quick Start
text: Spatial Workflows – boundary constraints, barrier models, sf integration Spherical Temporal Meshes – global meshes, space-time, metric graphs

title: Spatial Workflows
text: default, tulpa_mesh() uses convex hull extended 10%. can provide explicit boundary coordinate matrix:
code: set.seed(42) coords <- cbind(runif(80, 1, 9), runif(80, 1, 9))  # L-shaped boundary bnd <- rbind(   c(0, 0), c(10, 0), c(10, 5),   c(5, 5), c(5, 10), c(0, 10) )  mesh <- tulpa_mesh(coords, boundary = bnd, max_edge = 1, extend = 0) plot(mesh, vertex_col = "black", main = "L-shaped domain")

title: Spatial Workflows
text: Pass sf polygon directly. CRS preserved automatically.
code: library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.13.1, GDAL 3.11.4, PROJ 9.7.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE  poly <- st_polygon(list(rbind(   c(0, 0), c(10, 0), c(10, 10), c(0, 10), c(0, 0) ))) sfc <- st_sfc(poly, crs = 32633)  mesh_sf <- tulpa_mesh(coords, boundary = sfc, max_edge = 1, extend = 0) mesh_crs(mesh_sf)  # CRS attached #> Coordinate Reference System: #>   User input: EPSG:32633  #>   wkt: #> PROJCRS["WGS 84 / UTM zone 33N", #>     BASEGEOGCRS["WGS 84", #>         ENSEMBLE["World Geodetic System 1984 ensemble", #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (Transit)"], #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (G730)"], #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (G873)"], #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (G1150)"], #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (G1674)"], #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (G1762)"], #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (G2139)"], #>             MEMBER["World Geodetic System 1984 (G2296)"], #>             ELLIPSOID["WGS 84",6378137,298.257223563, #>                 LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]], #>             ENSEMBLEACCURACY[2.0]], #>         PRIMEM["Greenwich",0, #>             ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]], #>         ID["EPSG",4326]], #>     CONVERSION["UTM zone 33N", #>         METHOD["Transverse Mercator", #>             ID["EPSG",9807]], #>         PARAMETER["Latitude of natural origin",0, #>             ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433], #>             ID["EPSG",8801]], #>         PARAMETER["Longitude of natural origin",15, #>             ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433], #>             ID["EPSG",8802]], #>         PARAMETER["Scale factor at natural origin",0.9996, #>             SCALEUNIT["unity",1], #>             ID["EPSG",8805]], #>         PARAMETER["False easting",500000, #>             LENGTHUNIT["metre",1], #>             ID["EPSG",8806]], #>         PARAMETER["False northing",0, #>             LENGTHUNIT["metre",1], #>             ID["EPSG",8807]]], #>     CS[Cartesian,2], #>         AXIS["(E)",east, #>             ORDER[1], #>             LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]], #>         AXIS["(N)",north, #>             ORDER[2], #>             LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]], #>     USAGE[ #>         SCOPE["Navigation and medium accuracy spatial referencing."], #>         AREA["Between 12°E and 18°E, northern hemisphere between equator and 84°N, onshore and offshore. Austria. Bosnia and Herzegovina. Cameroon. Central African Republic. Chad. Congo. Croatia. Czechia. Democratic Republic of the Congo (Zaire). Gabon. Germany. Hungary. Italy. Libya. Malta. Niger. Nigeria. Norway. Poland. San Marino. Slovakia. Slovenia. Svalbard. Sweden. Vatican City State."], #>         BBOX[0,12,84,18]], #>     ID["EPSG",32633]]

title: Spatial Workflows
text: Holes sf polygons become constraint edges mesh respects:
code: outer <- rbind(c(0, 0), c(10, 0), c(10, 10), c(0, 10), c(0, 0)) hole  <- rbind(c(3, 3), c(7, 3), c(7, 7), c(3, 7), c(3, 3)) poly_h <- st_polygon(list(outer, hole))  # Remove points inside the hole inside <- coords[, 1] > 3 & coords[, 1] < 7 & coords[, 2] > 3 & coords[, 2] < 7 pts_outside <- coords[!inside, ]  mesh_h <- tulpa_mesh(pts_outside, boundary = st_sfc(poly_h), max_edge = 1, extend = 0) plot(mesh_h, vertex_col = "black", main = "Mesh with hole")

title: Spatial Workflows
text: Physical barriers (coastlines, rivers) prevent spatial field smoothing across . Mark barrier triangles pass fem_matrices():
code:
set.seed(42) coords <- cbind(runif(100, 0, 10), runif(100, 0, 10)) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(coords, max_edge = 0.8)  # A river running through the domain river <- rbind(c(4, 0), c(6, 5), c(4, 10)) bt <- barrier_triangles(mesh, river)  cat(sum(bt), "barrier triangles out of", mesh$n_triangles, "
") #> 56 barrier triangles out of 579  # FEM with barrier: stiffness is zeroed for barrier triangles fem_b <- fem_matrices(mesh, barrier = bt)  # Compare: barrier mesh has fewer stiffness nonzeros fem_n <- fem_matrices(mesh) cat("Nonzeros without barrier:", length(fem_n$G@x), "
") #> Nonzeros without barrier: 2047 cat("Nonzeros with barrier:   ", length(fem_b$G@x), "
") #> Nonzeros with barrier:    1905

title: Spatial Workflows
text: Split every triangle 4 uniform refinement:
code: set.seed(42) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(cbind(runif(20), runif(20))) sub <- subdivide_mesh(mesh) cat("Original:", mesh$n_triangles, "triangles\n") #> Original: 46 triangles cat("Subdivided:", sub$n_triangles, "triangles\n") #> Subdivided: 184 triangles

title: Spatial Workflows
text: Refine error indicators high. typical workflow initial SPDE solve: solver returns posterior variance per triangle, refine variance large.
code: set.seed(42) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(cbind(runif(50), runif(50)), max_edge = 0.15)  # Simulate error indicators (high in one corner) q <- mesh_quality(mesh) centroids <- cbind(   (mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,1], 1] + mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,2], 1] +    mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,3], 1]) / 3,   (mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,1], 2] + mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,2], 2] +    mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,3], 2]) / 3 ) error <- exp(-3 * centroids[, 1])  # high error near x = 0  refined <- refine_mesh(mesh, error, fraction = 0.3) cat("Before:", mesh$n_triangles, "triangles\n") #> Before: 211 triangles cat("After: ", refined$n_triangles, "triangles\n") #> After:  370 triangles

title: Spatial Workflows
code: set.seed(42) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(cbind(runif(50, 0, 10), runif(50, 0, 10)), max_edge = 1)  # Keep only left half q <- mesh_quality(mesh) centroids_x <- (mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,1], 1] +                 mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,2], 1] +                 mesh$vertices[mesh$triangles[,3], 1]) / 3 left <- subset_mesh(mesh, centroids_x < 5) left #> tulpa_mesh: #>   Vertices:   86  #>   Triangles:  144  #>   Edges:      229

title: Spatial Workflows
code: comps <- mesh_components(mesh) cat("Number of components:", max(comps), "\n") #> Number of components: 1

title: Spatial Workflows
text: existing fmesher mesh, convert directly: FEM matrices converted mesh match fmesher’s output exactly.
code: library(fmesher) #> Warning: package 'fmesher' was built under R version 4.5.3 fm <- fm_mesh_2d(loc = coords, max.edge = c(1, 3)) tm <- as_tulpa_mesh(fm) tm #> tulpa_mesh: #>   Vertices:   507  #>   Triangles:  965  #>   Edges:      1471

title: CLAUDE.md
text: file provides guidance Claude Code (claude.ai/code) working code repository.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R package constrained Delaunay triangulation mesh generation FEM matrix assembly spatial SPDE models. Hard dependency tulpa Bayesian hierarchical modeling engine. Built CDT header-C++ library (vendored src/cdt/, MPL-2.0, documented inst/COPYRIGHTS).

title: CLAUDE.md
code: # Load package (compile C++ and source R) "C:/Program Files/R/R-4.5.2/bin/Rscript.exe" -e 'devtools::load_all()'  # Run tests (141 tests) "C:/Program Files/R/R-4.5.2/bin/Rscript.exe" -e 'devtools::test()'  # Run single test file "C:/Program Files/R/R-4.5.2/bin/Rscript.exe" -e 'devtools::test(filter = "sphere")'  # Full R CMD check "C:/Program Files/R/R-4.5.2/bin/Rscript.exe" -e 'devtools::check(args = "--no-manual")'  # After editing C++ files: regenerate Rcpp exports then rebuild docs "C:/Program Files/R/R-4.5.2/bin/Rscript.exe" -e 'Rcpp::compileAttributes(); devtools::document()'

title: CLAUDE.md
code: User coordinates + boundary         │         ▼ tulpa_mesh()  [R/mesh.R]          Formula parsing, convex hull extension,         │                          boundary constraints, grid refinement,         │                          deduplication, Ruppert refinement         ├─► cpp_triangulate()      CDT constrained Delaunay         └─► cpp_ruppert_refine()   Iterative circumcenter insertion         │         ▼ tulpa_mesh S3 object               vertices, triangles, edges (all 1-based)         │         ▼ fem_matrices()  [R/mesh.R]        Sparse matrix assembly         │         ├─► cpp_fem_matrices()     2D P1 FEM: mass (C) + stiffness (G)         ├─► cpp_fem_matrices_3d()  3D surface FEM (spherical meshes)         ├─► cpp_projection_matrix()   Grid-accelerated barycentric (2D)         └─► cpp_projection_matrix_3d() 3D surface projection         │         ▼ List(C, G, A, ...)                dgCMatrix sparse matrices

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Six // [[Rcpp::export]] functions: - cpp_triangulate() — CDT constrained Delaunay, erases outer/holes - cpp_ruppert_refine() — Ruppert algorithm min angle + max area + segment encroachment - cpp_fem_matrices() — 2D consistent mass + gradient stiffness (triplet format) - cpp_fem_matrices_3d() — 3D surface FEM via metric tensor tangent plane - cpp_projection_matrix() — grid-accelerated barycentric (O(n_obs × avg_cell) vs O(n_obs × n_tri)) - cpp_projection_matrix_3d() — 3D barycentric via Cramer’s rule Shared helpers: BaryResult barycentric(), TriGrid uniform spatial index, circumcenter(), min_angle_from_lengths().

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R input 1-based → converted 0-based CDT → output converted back 1-based. FEM triplets returned 0-based C++ converted R Matrix::sparseMatrix().

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Degenerate triangle/barycentric: < 1e-15 Point-inside boundary (2D): >= -1e-8 Point-inside boundary (3D): >= -1e-6 (sphere curvature) Zero-weight skip projection: < 1e-15

title: CLAUDE.md
text: 141 tests across 8 test files (testthat edition 3):

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Ruppert refinement: available via min_angle parameter. Iterative circumcenter insertion segment encroachment handling. Consistent mass matrix: standard SPDE. Lumped mass (diagonal C0) available via lumped = TRUE. Grid-accelerated projection: uniform grid spatial index replaces brute-force. Fallback full scan edge cases. Barrier models: barrier_triangles() identifies barrier regions; fem_matrices(barrier=) zeros stiffness contributions. Spherical meshes: icosahedral subdivision 3D FEM via metric tensor. Vertices exact sphere surface. Triplet accumulation: C++ returns (, j, x) vectors; R assembles dgCMatrix repr = "C".

title: CLAUDE.md
text: tulpaMesh → tulpa (SPDE Q-builder, Laplace solver) → tulpaOcc (occupancy models spatial fields). Positioned CRAN-publishable alternative fmesher/INLA mesh tooling.

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: installation guide focused development. regular installation, please see README.

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Clone tulpaMesh repository:
code: git clone https://github.com/gcol33/tulpaMesh.git cd tulpaMesh

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: tulpaMesh R package uses C++17 code via Rcpp RcppParallel. Install required tools R (>= 4.1) Rtools (Windows) Xcode Command Line Tools (macOS) Git editor IDE (RStudio, VS Code, etc.) Install development dependencies Load development build
code: install.packages(c("devtools", "roxygen2", "testthat", "Rcpp",                     "RcppParallel", "Matrix", "rmarkdown", "knitr")) devtools::load_all()

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Build install package locally: modify C++ code, regenerate Rcpp exports rebuilding:
code: devtools::install() Rcpp::compileAttributes() devtools::document() devtools::install()

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: tulpaMesh uses testthat (edition 3) testing. tests located tests/testthat/. Run full test suite: Run complete package check: Run single test file: Guidelines: - Keep tests fast reproducible. - Use set.seed() random data. - Include edge cases expected failures. - Prefer small examples large datasets.
code: devtools::test() devtools::check() devtools::test(filter = "sphere")

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Build vignettes: Build pkgdown site locally:
code: devtools::build_vignettes() source("~/.R/build_pkgdown.R") build_pkgdown_site()

title: Contribution Guidelines
code: tulpaMesh/ ├── .github/workflows/      <- CI (R-CMD-check, test-coverage) ├── R/                       <- R source files │   ├── mesh.R               <- tulpa_mesh(), fem_matrices() │   ├── sphere.R             <- tulpa_mesh_sphere(), print.tulpa_mesh() │   ├── mesh1d.R             <- tulpa_mesh_1d() │   ├── graph.R              <- tulpa_mesh_graph() │   ├── diagnostics.R        <- mesh_quality(), mesh_summary(), plot() │   ├── barrier.R            <- barrier_triangles() │   ├── convert.R            <- as_tulpa_mesh() │   ├── refine.R             <- refine_mesh() │   ├── mesh_ops.R           <- subdivide, components, subset │   ├── fem_p2.R             <- fem_matrices_p2() │   ├── nonstationary.R      <- fem_matrices_nonstationary() │   └── crs.R                <- mesh_crs(), set_crs() ├── src/                     <- C++ source │   ├── mesh.cpp             <- All Rcpp exports │   └── cdt/                 <- Vendored CDT library (MPL-2.0) ├── tests/testthat/          <- Unit tests (444 tests) ├── inst/                    <- CITATION, COPYRIGHTS, WORDLIST ├── man/                     <- Generated documentation (roxygen2) ├── DESCRIPTION ├── NAMESPACE ├── NEWS.md ├── README.md └── CLAUDE.md                <- Dev instructions for Claude Code

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Create feature branch Make focused commits clear messages. Run tests checks committing: Update documentation roxygen2 NEWS.md. Update vignettes/examples user-facing behavior changes. Open pull request short description change. Respond review feedback constructively.
code: git checkout -b feature/my-feature devtools::test() devtools::check()

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Use descriptive names consistent indentation. Prefer vectorized operations loops. Validate inputs early clear error messages. Document exported functions roxygen2.

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Keep headers minimal separate interface implementation. Use RAII possible. Comment algorithmic details numerical behavior. Avoid unnecessary memory allocations. Use Rcpp::stop(), never Rf_error().

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Add update tests functionality changes. Keep tests minimal reproducible. Avoid external dependencies unless essential.

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: Tests pass (devtools::test() devtools::check()) Documentation updated (roxygen + NEWS.md) Vignettes/examples updated needed unrelated formatting changes PR description clearly explains change

title: Contribution Guidelines
text: reporting issue, please include: - minimal reproducible example (reprex) - Output sessionInfo() - Expected vs. actual results - R operating system version - Toolchain info relevant (e.g., Rtools Windows) contributing tulpaMesh, agree code released license package.

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
code: library(tulpaMesh)  coords <- cbind(runif(200), runif(200)) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(coords, max_edge = 0.1) fem  <- fem_matrices(mesh, obs_coords = coords) # fem$C (mass), fem$G (stiffness), fem$A (projection) ready for SPDE

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
text: Spatial models based SPDE approach (Lindgren, Rue & Lindstrom 2011) need triangulated mesh finite element matrices. tulpaMesh handles mesh generation, quality refinement, FEM matrix assembly external dependencies beyond Rcpp Matrix. Common applications: Species distribution models spatial random effects Geostatistical models Matern covariance via SPDE Occupancy models spatially structured detection Bayesian hierarchical model needing spatial mesh

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
text: tulpa_mesh() - Constrained Delaunay points, formulas, sf boundaries tulpa_mesh_sphere() - Icosahedral geodesic meshes global-scale models tulpa_mesh_1d() - Temporal meshes space-time SPDE tulpa_mesh_graph() - Network meshes river/road SPDE

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
text: fem_matrices() - Mass (C), stiffness (G), projection () matrices fem_matrices(lumped = TRUE) - Diagonal mass SPDE Q-builder fem_matrices(parallel = TRUE) - Multithreaded assembly via TBB fem_matrices(barrier = ...) - Barrier models (Bakka et al. 2019) fem_matrices_p2() - Quadratic (P2) elements higher accuracy fem_matrices_nonstationary() - Spatially varying range variance

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
text: mesh_quality() - Per-triangle angles, aspect ratio, area mesh_summary() - Min/median/max quality statistics plot() - Mesh visualization optional quality coloring Ruppert refinement via min_angle max_area parameters

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
code: library(tulpaMesh)  set.seed(42) coords <- cbind(runif(100), runif(100)) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(coords, min_angle = 25, max_edge = 0.15) mesh_summary(mesh) plot(mesh, color = "quality", vertex_col = "black")

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
code: library(sf)  # Barrier polygon (e.g., land mass that field can't cross) barrier <- st_polygon(list(rbind(c(3,3), c(7,3), c(7,7), c(3,7), c(3,3)))) bt <- barrier_triangles(mesh, st_sfc(barrier)) fem <- fem_matrices(mesh, barrier = bt) # fem$G has zero stiffness across barrier triangles

title: tulpaMesh: Spatial Meshes and FEM Matrices for SPDE Models
code: globe <- tulpa_mesh_sphere(subdivisions = 4) globe #> tulpa_mesh (sphere, radius = 1): #>   Vertices:   2562 #>   Triangles:  5120 #>   Edges:      7680 fem <- fem_matrices(globe, lumped = TRUE) # Total surface area approximates 4*pi sum(fem$va)

title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Date: 2026-03-25 Status: Scaffolded, compiles, 22 tests passing Role: Hard dependency tulpa SPDE spatial fields. Also usable standalone spatial triangulation task.

title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: CDT header-C++ library vendored src/cdt/ (MIT, Artem Amirkhanov) cpp_triangulate(): constrained Delaunay triangulation points + optional edge constraints cpp_fem_matrices(): linear P1 FEM assembly (mass C, stiffness G) mesh triangles cpp_projection_matrix(): barycentric interpolation matrix mapping mesh nodes → observation locations tulpa_mesh(): R API formula interface, convex hull extension, boundary constraints, max_edge refinement, cutoff deduplication fem_matrices(): R wrapper returning sparse dgCMatrix objects (via Matrix package) 22 tests passing

title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
code: User coordinates + boundary         ↓ tulpaMesh::tulpa_mesh()     ← R API         ↓ calls cpp_triangulate()           ← CDT C++ (constrained Delaunay)         ↓ returns tulpa_mesh object           ← vertices, triangles, edges         ↓ tulpaMesh::fem_matrices()   ← R API         ↓ calls cpp_fem_matrices()          ← C++ FEM assembly cpp_projection_matrix()     ← C++ barycentric projection         ↓ returns Sparse C, G, A matrices    ← dgCMatrix (Matrix package)         ↓ tulpa SPDE Q-builder        ← lives in tulpa, not here

previous_headings: Remaining Work > P0: Must-do before tulpa integration
title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: src/mesh.cpp, R/mesh.R : current mesh refinement quality guarantees. Need: - Minimum angle constraint (Delaunay refinement à la Ruppert/Chew). CDT doesn’t natively — ’s constrained Delaunay triangulator, mesh generator quality guarantees. Options: - Post-process: insert Steiner points circumcenters bad triangles (angle < threshold), re-triangulate. Ruppert algorithm can implemented top CDT. - : accept SPDE mesh quality CDT + max_edge refinement good enough (usually — INLA’s fmesher doesn’t guarantee minimum angles either, just CDT + refinement). - Decision needed: Implement Ruppert refinement accept CDT + max_edge sufficient? v0.1 ’d say accept CDT quality add Ruppert later users hit issues.

title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: tulpa’s DESCRIPTION, tulpa’s R/spatial.R : - Add tulpaMesh tulpa’s Imports - Create spatial_spde() tulpa : 1. Takes coords (formula + data) + optional boundary 2. Calls tulpaMesh::tulpa_mesh() build mesh 3. Calls tulpaMesh::fem_matrices() get C, G, 4. Stores SPDE Q-builder - Create SPDE Laplace mode function tulpa’s laplace_core.cpp: - Latent field w lives mesh nodes (n_mesh elements) - η = Xβ + ·w (projection mesh observations) - Q(κ) = κ⁴C + 2κ²G + GC⁻¹G (classical SPDE precision) - Priors w: -0.5 w’Qw - new variant laplace_newton_solve scatter callback maps

previous_headings: Remaining Work > P1: Classical SPDE in tulpa
title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: tulpa src/spde_qbuilder.h, src/spde_qbuilder.cpp : Given sparse C, G matrices hyperparameters (range ρ, variance σ²): - κ = √(8·ν) / ρ (ν = 1, α = 2 2D) - τ² = 1 / (4π · κ² · σ²) - Q = τ² · (κ⁴·C + 2κ²·G + G·C⁻¹·G) - C⁻¹ diagonal (lumped mass matrix) G·C⁻¹·G sparse - Key property: sparsity pattern Q fixed (C + G pattern). values change θ. means CHOLMOD analyze() , factorize() per grid point. - Store C, G cholmod_sparse* direct CHOLMOD operations - Implement void build_spde_precision(double kappa, double tau, cholmod_sparse* C, cholmod_sparse* G, cholmod_sparse* Q) — fills Q values -place

previous_headings: Remaining Work > P1: Classical SPDE in tulpa
what: 7. SPDE Laplace mode function in tulpa
title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: tulpa src/laplace_core.cpp : New laplace_mode_spde() variant: Latent field dimension = n_mesh (n_obs) η_i = X_i·β + Σ_j A_{ij}·w_j Scatter: gradient Hessian contributions mapped ’ (transpose projection) Prior: -0.5·w’·Q(κ,τ)·w, gradient = -Q·w, Hessian = Q Hessian H = ’·diag(h_i)·+ Q — sparse! CHOLMOD handles full sparse solve
code: LaplaceResult laplace_mode_spde(     y, n_trials, X,     A_sparse,          // projection matrix (n_obs × n_mesh), cholmod_sparse     C_sparse, G_sparse, // FEM matrices, cholmod_sparse     kappa, tau,         // SPDE hyperparameters     family, phi, max_iter, tol, n_threads,     x_init, shared_solver );

previous_headings: Remaining Work > P1: Classical SPDE in tulpa
what: 8. Nested Laplace for SPDE (2D grid over range and variance)
title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: tulpa src/laplace_core.cpp, R/nested_laplace.R : cpp_nested_laplace_spde() — C++ grid loop (ρ, σ²) pairs: - CCD grid k=2 hyperparameters → 9 grid points - point: rebuild Q (ρ, σ²), run inner Laplace, warm-start previous - CHOLMOD: analyze (Q sparsity (ρ, σ²)), factorize per grid point - “INLA replacement” continuous spatial fields

previous_headings: Remaining Work > P1: Classical SPDE in tulpa
title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: tulpa R/spatial.R :
code: spatial_spde <- function(coords, data = NULL, mesh = NULL,                          boundary = NULL, max_edge = NULL,                          nu = 1, prior_range = NULL, prior_sigma = NULL) {   # If mesh not provided, build one   if (is.null(mesh)) {     mesh <- tulpaMesh::tulpa_mesh(coords, data = data,                                    boundary = boundary, max_edge = max_edge)   }   # Compute FEM matrices   obs_coords <- resolve_coords(coords, data)   fem <- tulpaMesh::fem_matrices(mesh, obs_coords = obs_coords)    structure(list(     type = "spde", mesh = mesh,     C = fem$C, G = fem$G, A = fem$A,     n_mesh = fem$n_mesh, nu = nu,     prior_range = prior_range, prior_sigma = prior_sigma   ), class = "tulpa_spatial") }

previous_headings: Remaining Work > P2: Rational SPDE (the differentiator)
title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: tulpa src/spde_qbuilder.h : Bolin, Simas & Xiong (2023) rational approximation: - fractional α = ν + d/2 (d=2 2D), precision operator (κ² - Δ)^α can’t represented exactly classical SPDE stencil - Rational approximation: P_m(L) ≈ L^α L = κ²C + G P_m degree-m rational polynomial - Q = P_m’ · C · P_m - polynomial coefficients come best rational approximation (Padé Chebyshev) - Advantage: arbitrary ν > 0, just ν ∈ {1, 2}. Users can estimate ν data. - Implementation: Pre-compute rational polynomial coefficients given m (typically m=2 m=3 sufficient). Build Q product sparse matrices. - Reference: rSPDE package David Bolin (R implementation exists, ’d reimplement Q-builder C++ CHOLMOD integration)

previous_headings: Remaining Work > P2: Rational SPDE (the differentiator)
title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Files: tulpa R/nested_laplace.R : rational SPDE, ν becomes hyperparameter can estimated: - 3D grid (range, variance, smoothness) — CCD k=3 → 15 grid points - : fix ν 2D grid, profile ν - INLA can’t natively — tulpa first CRAN package offer nested Laplace estimated Matérn smoothness

title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: User sees: hood: 1. spatial_spde() calls tulpaMesh::tulpa_mesh() → mesh 2. spatial_spde() calls tulpaMesh::fem_matrices() → C, G, 3. occ() calls tulpa’s cpp_laplace_fit_spde() cpp_nested_laplace_spde() → fit 4. CHOLMOD handles sparse solves
code: tulpaMesh (mesh + FEM)     ↓ hard dep tulpa (SPDE Q-builder + CHOLMOD + nested Laplace)     ↓ hard dep tulpaOcc (occupancy models with SPDE spatial fields) library(tulpaOcc) fit <- occ(~ elevation, ~ 1, data = survey_data,            spatial = spatial_spde(coords = ~ x + y, max_edge = c(0.5, 2)))

title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: Key differentiator: tulpa CRAN-available engine offering rational SPDE + nested Laplace + estimated Matérn smoothness + occupancy models single install.

title: tulpaMesh Development Plan
text: CDT license CRAN context: CDT MPL-2.0. vendored headers used compile time (header-). Need verify MPL-2.0 compatible MIT (tulpaMesh) CRAN. MPL-2.0 allows “larger work” different license long MPL files retain license. fine — document inst/COPYRIGHTS LICENSE.note. Consistent vs lumped mass matrix: Currently using consistent mass (area/6 diagonal, area/12 -diagonal). SPDE Q-builder needs C⁻¹ — lumped mass (diagonal) makes trivial. Consistent mass accurate requires solving sparse system C⁻¹·G. Decision: use lumped mass Q-builder (standard practice INLA), keep consistent mass available uses. Projection matrix point location: Current cpp_projection_matrix brute-force triangle search (O(n_obs × n_tri)). large meshes, need spatial index (KD-tree triangle walk). CDT’s KDTree.h already vendored — use point location. blocking v0.1 needed n_obs > 10K. fmesher compatibility: tulpaMesh able import fmesher meshes? let users switch INLA without re-meshing. Low priority good adoption. simple as_tulpa_mesh.fm_mesh_2d() converter suffice. Matrix package version: fem_matrices() uses Matrix::sparseMatrix(). Need Matrix (>= 1.5-0) repr argument. Add version constraint Imports.

title: Identify Barrier Triangles — barrier_triangles
text: Determines mesh triangles fall inside barrier regions (e.g., coastlines, rivers, lakes). triangle marked barrier centroid falls inside barrier polygon.

title: Identify Barrier Triangles — barrier_triangles
code: barrier_triangles(mesh, barriers)

title: Identify Barrier Triangles — barrier_triangles
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object. barriers sf/sfc object defining barrier regions, list N x 2 coordinate matrices (defining closed polygon).

title: Identify Barrier Triangles — barrier_triangles
text: logical vector length n_triangles. TRUE indicates triangle inside barrier region.

what: Compute FEM Matrices from a Mesh — fem_matrices
title: Compute FEM Matrices from a Mesh — fem_matrices
text: Computes finite element mass (C) stiffness (G) matrices triangular mesh, plus projection matrix () maps mesh vertices observation locations.

title: Compute FEM Matrices from a Mesh — fem_matrices
code: fem_matrices(   mesh,   obs_coords = NULL,   barrier = NULL,   parallel = FALSE,   lumped = FALSE )

title: Compute FEM Matrices from a Mesh — fem_matrices
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object. obs_coords Observation coordinates (N x 2 matrix). NULL, projection matrix identity (observations mesh nodes). barrier Optional logical vector length n_triangles. Triangles marked TRUE treated physical barriers (coastlines, rivers): stiffness contributions zeroed spatial field smooth across . Based Bakka et al. (2019). parallel Logical. TRUE, uses parallel FEM assembly via RcppParallel (thread-local triplet accumulation). Beneficial meshes >50K triangles. Default FALSE. lumped Logical. TRUE, returns diagonal lumped mass matrix C0 (vertex areas) addition consistent mass matrix C. lumped mass inverse trivial needed SPDE Q-builder. Default FALSE.

title: Compute FEM Matrices from a Mesh — fem_matrices
text: list sparse matrices (dgCMatrix class Matrix package): C: consistent mass matrix (n_vertices x n_vertices) G: stiffness matrix (n_vertices x n_vertices) : projection matrix (n_obs x n_vertices) n_mesh: number mesh vertices C0: (lumped = TRUE) diagonal lumped mass matrix va: (lumped = TRUE) vertex areas (numeric vector) ta: (lumped = TRUE) triangle areas (numeric vector)

what: Non-stationary FEM Matrices with Spatially Varying Parameters — fem_matrices_nonstationary
title: Non-stationary FEM Matrices with Spatially Varying Parameters — fem_matrices_nonstationary
text: Computes weighted FEM matrices non-stationary SPDE models range variance parameters vary spatially. weights per-vertex kappa(s) tau(s) values, interpolated within triangle using element-average approximation.

title: Non-stationary FEM Matrices with Spatially Varying Parameters — fem_matrices_nonstationary
code: fem_matrices_nonstationary(mesh, kappa, tau)

title: Non-stationary FEM Matrices with Spatially Varying Parameters — fem_matrices_nonstationary
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object (2D ). kappa Numeric vector length n_vertices giving spatial scale parameter kappa(s) = sqrt(8*nu) / range(s). tau Numeric vector length n_vertices giving precision scaling tau(s).

title: Non-stationary FEM Matrices with Spatially Varying Parameters — fem_matrices_nonstationary
text: list sparse matrices: Ck: kappa²-weighted mass matrix Gk: kappa²-weighted stiffness matrix Ct: tau²-weighted mass matrix C: unweighted consistent mass matrix G: unweighted stiffness matrix C0: lumped (diagonal) mass matrix n_mesh: number mesh vertices

title: Compute P2 (Quadratic) FEM Matrices — fem_matrices_p2
text: Computes finite element matrices using 6-node quadratic triangular elements. triangle edge gets midpoint node, giving 6 basis functions per triangle instead 3. Quadratic elements provide better approximation accuracy fewer mesh nodes.

title: Compute P2 (Quadratic) FEM Matrices — fem_matrices_p2
code: fem_matrices_p2(mesh)

title: Compute P2 (Quadratic) FEM Matrices — fem_matrices_p2
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object (2D ).

title: Compute P2 (Quadratic) FEM Matrices — fem_matrices_p2
text: list : C: consistent mass matrix (n_total x n_total sparse) G: stiffness matrix (n_total x n_total sparse) n_mesh: total number nodes (vertices + midpoints) n_vertices: number original mesh vertices n_midpoints: number added midpoint nodes vertices: N x 2 matrix node coordinates triangles6: M x 6 connectivity matrix (columns: v0, v1, v2, mid01, mid12, mid20)

title: Print Mesh Quality Summary — mesh_summary
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object.

title: Print Mesh Quality Summary — mesh_summary
text: Invisible data.frame per-triangle quality metrics.

title: Plot a Triangular Mesh — plot.tulpa_mesh
text: Draws mesh using base R graphics: edges line segments, vertices points. Optionally colors triangles quality metric.

title: Plot a Triangular Mesh — plot.tulpa_mesh
code: # S3 method for class 'tulpa_mesh' plot(   x,   color = NULL,   border = "grey50",   vertex_col = NULL,   vertex_cex = 0.5,   palette = grDevices::hcl.colors,   n_colors = 100L,   main = "tulpa_mesh",   ... )

title: Plot a Triangular Mesh — plot.tulpa_mesh
text: x tulpa_mesh object. color Optional per-triangle numeric vector color triangles (e.g., output mesh_quality()$min_angle). "quality", uses minimum angle. NULL, draws edges . border Edge color. Default "grey50". vertex_col Vertex point color. Default NULL (vertices drawn). vertex_cex Vertex point size. Default 0.5. palette Color palette function triangle fill. Default grDevices::hcl.colors. n_colors Number colors palette. Default 100. main Plot title. ... Additional arguments passed plot.default().

title: Plot a Triangular Mesh — plot.tulpa_mesh
text: tulpa_mesh object x, returned invisibly. Called side effect producing plot.

what: Adaptively Refine a Mesh Based on Error Indicators — refine_mesh
title: Adaptively Refine a Mesh Based on Error Indicators — refine_mesh
text: Refines triangles error indicators exceed threshold inserting centroids new vertices re-triangulating. Designed iterative solve-refine-re-solve workflows error indicators come SPDE solver (e.g., tulpa's posterior variance).

title: Adaptively Refine a Mesh Based on Error Indicators — refine_mesh
code: refine_mesh(   mesh,   indicators,   threshold = NULL,   fraction = NULL,   max_iter = 1L,   min_area = 0 )

title: Adaptively Refine a Mesh Based on Error Indicators — refine_mesh
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object (2D ). indicators Numeric vector per-triangle error indicators (length n_triangles). Higher values trigger refinement. threshold Triangles indicator value refined. Default: median(indicators) (refine worst half). fraction Alternative threshold: refine fraction triangles highest indicators. Default NULL (use threshold). max_iter Maximum number refine-retriangulate iterations. Default 1 (single refinement pass). min_area Minimum triangle area triangles never refined, regardless indicator. Default 0 (limit).

title: Adaptively Refine a Mesh Based on Error Indicators — refine_mesh
text: new tulpa_mesh object refined triangulation.

title: Subdivide a Mesh — subdivide_mesh
text: Splits triangle 4 sub-triangles inserting edge midpoints, re-triangulates. Useful multi-resolution workflows coarser mesh needs uniform refinement.

title: Subdivide a Mesh — subdivide_mesh
code: subdivide_mesh(mesh)

title: Subdivide a Mesh — subdivide_mesh
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object (2D ).

title: Subdivide a Mesh — subdivide_mesh
text: new tulpa_mesh object approximately 4x many triangles.

what: Extract a Submesh from Triangle Indices — subset_mesh
title: Extract a Submesh from Triangle Indices — subset_mesh
text: Creates new tulpa_mesh containing specified triangles, vertex indices remapped.

title: Extract a Submesh from Triangle Indices — subset_mesh
code: subset_mesh(mesh, triangles)

title: Extract a Submesh from Triangle Indices — subset_mesh
text: mesh tulpa_mesh object. triangles Integer vector triangle indices keep, logical vector length n_triangles.

title: Extract a Submesh from Triangle Indices — subset_mesh
text: new tulpa_mesh object containing selected triangles vertices.

what: tulpaMesh: Constrained Delaunay Triangulation Meshes for Spatial 'SPDE' Models — tulpaMesh-package
title: tulpaMesh: Constrained Delaunay Triangulation Meshes for Spatial 'SPDE' Models — tulpaMesh-package
text: Generate constrained Delaunay triangulation meshes use stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) spatial models ('Lindgren', 'Rue' 'Lindstroem' 2011 doi:10.1111/j.1467-9868.2011.00777.x ). Provides automatic mesh generation point coordinates boundary constraints, 'Ruppert' refinement mesh quality, finite element method (FEM) matrix assembly (mass, stiffness, projection), barrier models, spherical meshes via icosahedral subdivision, metric graph meshes network geometries. Built 'CDT' header-'C++' library ('Amirkhanov' 2024 https://github.com/artem-ogre/CDT). Designed mesh backend 'tulpa' Bayesian hierarchical modelling engine usable standalone spatial triangulation task.

title: tulpaMesh: Constrained Delaunay Triangulation Meshes for Spatial 'SPDE' Models — tulpaMesh-package
text: Maintainer: Gilles Colling gilles.colling051@gmail.com (ORCID) [copyright holder]

what: Create a Triangular Mesh for SPDE Spatial Models — tulpa_mesh
title: Create a Triangular Mesh for SPDE Spatial Models — tulpa_mesh
text: Generates constrained Delaunay triangulation point coordinates, optionally boundary constraints. resulting mesh can used directly tulpa's SPDE spatial fields.

title: Create a Triangular Mesh for SPDE Spatial Models — tulpa_mesh
code: tulpa_mesh(   coords,   data = NULL,   boundary = NULL,   max_edge = NULL,   cutoff = 0,   extend = 0.1,   min_angle = NULL,   max_area = NULL,   max_steiner = 10000L )  # S3 method for class 'tulpa_mesh' print(x, ...)

title: Create a Triangular Mesh for SPDE Spatial Models — tulpa_mesh
text: coords matrix data.frame columns x y, formula like ~ x + y evaluated data. data Optional data.frame formula evaluation. boundary Optional boundary specification: matrix boundary vertex coordinates (N x 2), sf polygon, NULL convex hull. max_edge Maximum edge length. single value vector two values c(inner, outer) inner controls study area outer controls extension region. cutoff Minimum distance mesh vertices. Points closer merged. Default 0 (merging). extend Numeric extension factor beyond boundary. Default 0.1 (10% domain diameter). Set 0 extension. min_angle Minimum angle (degrees) Ruppert refinement. specified, Steiner points inserted circumcenters triangles angles threshold. Theoretical maximum ~20.7 degrees; values 30 usually work. Default NULL (refinement). max_area Maximum triangle area refinement. Triangles larger refined regardless angle quality. Default NULL (area constraint). max_steiner Maximum number Steiner points insert Ruppert refinement. Default 10000. x tulpa_mesh object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Create a Triangular Mesh for SPDE Spatial Models — tulpa_mesh
text: tulpa_mesh object components: vertices: N x 2 matrix vertex coordinates triangles: M x 3 integer matrix vertex indices (1-based) edges: K x 2 integer matrix edge vertex indices (1-based) n_vertices: number mesh vertices n_triangles: number triangles n_edges: number edges n_input_points: number original input points tulpa_mesh object x, returned invisibly.

title: Create a Triangular Mesh for SPDE Spatial Models — tulpa_mesh
code: # Simple mesh from random points set.seed(42) coords <- cbind(runif(50), runif(50)) mesh <- tulpa_mesh(coords) print(mesh)  # Mesh with Ruppert refinement (min angle 25 degrees) mesh_refined <- tulpa_mesh(coords, min_angle = 25)

what: Create a 1D Mesh for Temporal SPDE Models — tulpa_mesh_1d
title: Create a 1D Mesh for Temporal SPDE Models — tulpa_mesh_1d
text: Generates 1D mesh (interval partition) temporal components spatio-temporal SPDE models. Returns 1D FEM matrices (tridiagonal mass stiffness) can combined 2D spatial FEM matrices via Kronecker products tulpa's space-time Q-builder.

title: Create a 1D Mesh for Temporal SPDE Models — tulpa_mesh_1d
code: tulpa_mesh_1d(knots, boundary = NULL, n_extend = 3L)  # S3 method for class 'tulpa_mesh_1d' print(x, ...)

title: Create a 1D Mesh for Temporal SPDE Models — tulpa_mesh_1d
text: knots Numeric vector mesh knot locations (e.g., time points). sorted deduplicated. boundary Two-element numeric vector c(lower, upper) defining mesh domain. Defaults range(knots). n_extend Number extra knots add beyond boundary, spaced median knot interval. Default 3. x tulpa_mesh_1d object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Create a 1D Mesh for Temporal SPDE Models — tulpa_mesh_1d
text: tulpa_mesh_1d object components: knots: sorted numeric vector mesh knot locations n: number knots C: consistent mass matrix (tridiagonal, n x n sparse) G: stiffness matrix (tridiagonal, n x n sparse) C0: lumped (diagonal) mass matrix tulpa_mesh_1d object x, returned invisibly.

what: Create a Metric Graph Mesh — tulpa_mesh_graph
title: Create a Metric Graph Mesh — tulpa_mesh_graph
text: Builds 1D FEM mesh along edges spatial network (roads, rivers, coastlines). edge discretized segments P1 linear elements. Junction nodes edges meet shared. Based Whittle-Matern SPDE formulation metric graphs (Bolin, Simas & Wallin, 2024).

title: Create a Metric Graph Mesh — tulpa_mesh_graph
code: tulpa_mesh_graph(edges, max_edge = NULL, snap_tolerance = 1e-08)  # S3 method for class 'tulpa_mesh_graph' print(x, ...)

title: Create a Metric Graph Mesh — tulpa_mesh_graph
text: edges list N x 2 coordinate matrices, defining polyline edge network. sf object LINESTRING geometries. max_edge Maximum segment length along edges. Edges longer subdivided. Default NULL (use vertices -). snap_tolerance Distance endpoints snapped junction node. Default 1e-8. x tulpa_mesh_graph object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Create a Metric Graph Mesh — tulpa_mesh_graph
text: tulpa_mesh_graph object components: vertices: N x 2 matrix node coordinates segments: M x 2 integer matrix segment connectivity (1-based) n_vertices, n_segments: counts C: consistent mass matrix (tridiagonal-block sparse) G: stiffness matrix (tridiagonal-block sparse) C0: lumped (diagonal) mass matrix degree: integer vector node degrees (junction = degree > 2) tulpa_mesh_graph object x, returned invisibly.

what: Create a Triangular Mesh on the Sphere — tulpa_mesh_sphere
title: Create a Triangular Mesh on the Sphere — tulpa_mesh_sphere
text: Generates geodesic mesh unit sphere recursive subdivision icosahedron. Optionally refines around data locations using stereographic projection local Delaunay triangulation.

title: Create a Triangular Mesh on the Sphere — tulpa_mesh_sphere
code: tulpa_mesh_sphere(subdivisions = 3L, coords = NULL, radius = 1)

title: Create a Triangular Mesh on the Sphere — tulpa_mesh_sphere
text: subdivisions Number recursive icosahedral subdivisions. level quadruples triangle count: 0 = 20 triangles, 1 = 80, 2 = 320, 3 = 1280, 4 = 5120, 5 = 20480. Default 3. coords Optional matrix lon/lat coordinates (degrees) insert mesh. Points projected sphere added additional vertices via local re-triangulation. radius Sphere radius. Default 1 (unit sphere). Earth, use 6371 (km).

title: Create a Triangular Mesh on the Sphere — tulpa_mesh_sphere
text: tulpa_mesh object components: vertices: N x 3 matrix (x, y, z) Cartesian coordinates sphere surface triangles: M x 3 integer matrix vertex indices (1-based) edges: K x 2 integer matrix edge vertex indices (1-based) lonlat: N x 2 matrix (longitude, latitude) degrees n_vertices, n_triangles, n_edges: counts n_input_points: number original input points sphere: list radius subdivisions

title: tulpaMesh 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release.

title: tulpaMesh 0.1.0
text: tulpa_mesh(): constrained Delaunay triangulation formula interface, sf boundary support (POLYGON, MULTIPOLYGON, holes), hexagonal lattice refinement, Ruppert refinement (min_angle, max_area), vertex deduplication. tulpa_mesh_sphere(): icosahedral geodesic meshes 3D surface FEM. tulpa_mesh_1d(): temporal meshes tridiagonal FEM matrices. tulpa_mesh_graph(): metric graph meshes network SPDE models.

title: tulpaMesh 0.1.0
text: fem_matrices(): P1 linear FEM producing mass (C), stiffness (G), projection () sparse matrices. Supports lumped mass, barrier models, parallel assembly via RcppParallel. fem_matrices_p2(): P2 quadratic FEM 6-node triangular elements. fem_matrices_nonstationary(): spatially varying kappa/tau weighting.

title: tulpaMesh 0.1.0
text: mesh_quality(), mesh_summary(), plot.tulpa_mesh(): diagnostics. subdivide_mesh(), subset_mesh(), mesh_components(): mesh manipulation. refine_mesh(): adaptive refinement error indicators. as_tulpa_mesh(): convert fmesher/INLA meshes. barrier_triangles(): identify barrier regions polygons. mesh_crs(), set_crs(): CRS support.
